coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, characterization and thermophysical properties of PAG-Amine functionalized graphene quantum dots based nanosuspension

Gupta, Vinit
Dehury, Ranjit Kumar
Haque, Mahelaqua A.
Kumar, Rahul
Rashid, Abira
coredata.dc:description: This work presents the synthesis, characterization and evaluation of thermophysical properties of PAG (Polyalkylene glycol)-amine functionalized graphene quantum dots-based nanosuspension. The synthesis is carried out in two steps. In the first step graphene oxide is synthesized using Hummers method and in the next step synthesis of amine functionalized graphene quantum dots is done. The obtained quantum dots are further ultrasonicated in the Polyalkylene glycol resulting in varying concentrations of nanosuspension. For the characterization of graphene oxide Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) analysis is done, and for nanosuspension High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) and ultraviolet (UV)-spectrophotometer analysis is carried out. The thermophysical properties such as thermal conductivity and viscosity are examined using the Hamilton and Maxwell model which are further validated with experimental investigations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene Quantum Dots
Polyalkylene Glycol
Nanoparticles
Electron Microscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (b), (c) Morphological characterization of GO using FESEM (d) EDS analysis of synthesized GO.
(a) Synthesized amine functionalized PAG-based graphene quantum dots nanosuspension (b) HRTEM imaging of synthesized nanosuspension.
(a) Ultraviolet spectrophotometer analysis of synthesized nanosuspension (b) Thermal conductivity dependence on nanofluid concentration (c) Viscosity of synthesized nanosuspension.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, characterization and thermophysical properties of PAG-Amine functionalized graphene quantum dots based nanosuspension

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, S.B. Jain Institute of Technology, Management and Research, Nagpur 441501, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Gandhinagar 382355, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents the synthesis, characterization and evaluation of thermophysical properties of PAG (Polyalkylene glycol)-amine functionalized graphene quantum dots-based nanosuspension. The synthesis is carried out in two steps. In the first step graphene oxide is synthesized using Hummers method and in the next step synthesis of amine functionalized graphene quantum dots is done. The obtained quantum dots are further ultrasonicated in the Polyalkylene glycol resulting in varying concentrations of nanosuspension. For the characterization of graphene oxide Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) analysis is done, and for nanosuspension High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) and ultraviolet (UV)-spectrophotometer analysis is carried out. The thermophysical properties such as thermal conductivity and viscosity are examined using the Hamilton and Maxwell model which are further validated with experimental investigations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The zero-dimensional quantum confinement regime applies to graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nanoparticles with crystallites smaller than 10 nm. In addition to graphene's significant surface area and electrical conductivity, quantum confinement and edge effects give them unique electronic and optical properties [1–3]. GQDs are found to be significant for optoelectronics, sensing, and biomedicine. In the latest research, the quantum dots are also being utilized to enhance the heat transfer capacity of the conventional fluid [2,4,5]. In recent years it is observed that the utilization of quantum dots improves the overall thermophysical properties of the conventional fluid. In the current work, the amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots are synthesized in Polyalkylene Glycol (PAG) and characterized. Further, the thermophysical properties of this mixture are evaluated experimentally and validated.

The synthesis method for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporates a two-step process where graphene oxide (GO) is first synthesized using Hummers' method followed by the functionalization with amine groups [6]. This functionalization is not merely a replication of existing methods but includes an innovative approach of using both nitric and sulfuric acid in the nitric steam method for enhanced functionalization [7]. The use of ultrasonication during the synthesis process enhances the uniformity and distribution of functional groups on the GQDs, which is critical for their application in enhancing the thermophysical properties of nanofluids.

To validate the thermophysical properties of the synthesized nanosuspension, mainly thermal conductivity and viscosity Hamilton model and Maxwell model are utilized (Eqs. (1) and (2). For the experimentation, a KD2 thermal property analyzer is utilized and a rotational viscometer is used for the viscosity evaluation.

The colloidal stability is observed for the synthesized PAG-amine functionalized GQD nanosuspension (Fig. 3 (a)). The stability of a nanosuspension can often be inferred from changes in the absorption spectrum over time. Allowing the nanosuspension to stabilize for 48 hrs. is aimed at assessing the medium-term stability of the suspension, which is more indicative of its behaviour in practical applications than its initial state immediately after preparation. Waiting for 48 hrs. also helps to ensure that any micro-bubbles formed during the preparation process have dissipated and that the quantum dots have reached a state of equilibrium within the suspension.

The UV spectrophotoscopy is done after 48 hrs. and further examined after 2 months. The absorbance peak in the range of 200–300 nm for the 100 ppm concentration shows a modest decrease after 2 months in comparison to the data taken after 48 h. This indicates a decrease in the amount of light absorbed, which may be caused by the clumping together of particles, their settling down, or the deterioration of the nanosuspension. Similarly, the 200 ppm sample exhibits a conspicuous reduction in the peak absorbance on comparing the 48 hrs. spectrum to the 2-month spectrum. This suggests a consistent trend with the 100 ppm sample, indicating decreased stability during the 2-month duration. The absorbance peak for the 500 ppm concentration exhibits a decline during the 2-month duration. Considering that the decrease is more noticeable compared to the 100 ppm and 200 ppm samples, it could indicate that higher concentrations are more vulnerable to time-dependent alterations, potentially caused by heightened particle interactions resulting in aggregation.

Thermal conductivity measurements using a KD2 thermal property analyzer for pure PAG oil and nanofluid concentrations of 100 ppm, 200 ppm, and 500 ppm are shown in Fig. 3(b). The test results showed a significant enhancement in thermal conductivity, with an increase of 35.29 % for the 100 ppm concentration, 54.71 % for the 200 ppm concentration, and 129.41 % for the 500 ppm concentration, compared to the thermal conductivity of the pure oil. The results are consistent with the expected pattern that the thermal conductivity of nanofluids increases as the concentration of nanoparticles increases. The increase in thermal conductivity can be due to the enhanced particle interactions within the base fluid as the concentration of GQDs increases. GQDs have high thermal conductivity due to their graphene-like structure, and their increased presence effectively forms a more interconnected thermal pathway throughout the fluid. This enhances the overall ability of the nanosuspension to conduct heat. To provide theoretical backing, Hamilton's model was utilized, which slightly underestimated the thermal conductivity in comparison to the experimental data, but exhibited a comparable upward trend.

From the experimentation as well as from the Maxwell model it is observed that (Fig. 3(c)) the viscosity remains consistently above that of the pure oil across the temperature range, indicating a persistent increase in viscosity with the addition of amine functionalized graphene quantum dots. As the concentration of GQDs increases, there is a corresponding increase in particle–particle interactions. These interactions can lead to a higher resistance to flow, as clusters or chains of particles form, which are harder to shear than individual particles or less concentrated suspensions. The classical Einstein equation for viscosity enhancement due to suspended particles highlights that viscosity increases with the volume fraction of the dispersed phase. The rise in viscosity suggests an enhanced ability of the lubricant to form a strong film at lower temperatures, perhaps leading to a decrease in mechanical wear in systems.

The current work investigates the synthesis, characterization and thermophysical properties of amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and their effect on PAG oil. The SEM images of graphene oxide display a surface that is rough and textured, with a layered structure. The HRTEM images of the graphene quantum dots treated with amines exhibit a homogeneous distribution and a consistent nanostructure, demonstrating high crystallinity and absence of defects. The combination of both imaging modalities confirms the successful production of graphene derivatives with characteristics that are suitable for advanced material applications. The thermal conductivity of PAG lubricating oil had a substantial increase with the incorporation of quantum dots nanoparticles. This improvement confirms the usefulness of nanoparticles in enhancing the heat transfer capabilities of fluids, which could have significant consequences in applications related to thermal management. The viscometer observations revealed that the presence of amine-functionalized quantum dots nanoparticles leads to an elevation in the viscosity of the nanosuspension at all examined concentrations. Subsequent research could concentrate on stabilizing agents or surface modifications to ensure the uniformity of dispersion is maintained over a prolonged period.

Yogesh G. Joshi: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Vinit Gupta: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ranjit Kumar Dehury: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Mahelaqua A. Haque: Software, Resources. Rahul Kumar: Validation, Data curation. Abira Rashid: Resources, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transport properties of graphene quantum dots in glycerol and distilled water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.K. Goharshadi, Z. Niyazi, M. Shafaee, M.B. Moghaddam, R. Ludwig, M. Namayandeh-Jorabchi, Transport properties of graphene quantum dots in glycerol and distilled water, J Mol Liq 241 (2017) 831–838. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2017.06.089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesizes, characterization, measurements and modeling thermal conductivity and viscosity of graphene quantum dots nanofluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Sedaghat, F. Yousefi, Synthesizes, characterization, measurements and modeling thermal conductivity and viscosity of graphene quantum dots nanofluids, J Mol Liq 278 (2019) 299–308. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2019.01.073.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of molten nitrate thermal properties by reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Hamdy, L. Saad, F. Abulfotuh, M. Soliman, S. Ebrahim, Enhancement of Molten Nitrate Thermal Properties by Reduced Graphene Oxide and Graphene Quantum Dots, ACS Omega 5 (2020) 21345–21354. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsomega.0c01291.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and thermophysical properties of functionalized graphene quantum dots for enhancing heat transfer of conventional fluid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.G. Joshi, D.R. Zanwar, A.A. Meshram, H. Fulekar, P. Gajghate, Synthesis and thermophysical properties of functionalized graphene quantum dots for enhancing heat transfer of conventional fluid, Mater Today Proc (2022). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matpr.2022.04.404.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and investigation of the heat transfer properties of a novel nanofluid based on graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Ettefaghi, B. Ghobadian, A. Rashidi, G. Najafi, M.H. Khoshtaghaza, S. Pourhashem, Preparation and investigation of the heat transfer properties of a novel nanofluid based on graphene quantum dots, Energy Convers Manag 153 (2017) 215–223. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enconman.2017.10.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transformer oils-based graphene quantum dots nanofluid as a new generation of highly conductive and stable coolant

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Amiri, M. Shanbedi, G. Ahmadi, S. Rozali, Transformer oils-based graphene quantum dots nanofluid as a new generation of highly conductive and stable coolant, International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 83 (2017) 40–47. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.icheatmasstransfer.2017.03.011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermophysical and rheological properties of water-based graphene quantum dots nanofluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Amiri, M. Shanbedi, H. Dashti, Thermophysical and rheological properties of water-based graphene quantum dots nanofluids, J Taiwan Inst Chem Eng 76 (2017) 132–140. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtice.2017.04.005.
